title: 2026/02/23 - SSC (Part 3)
displayTitle: Social Services and Community Committee - Social Security (Accident Compensation and Calculation of Weekly Income) Amendment Bill [Part 3]
topic: Social Security (Accident Compensation and Calculation of Weekly Income) Amendment Bill
description: Monday 23 February 2026 | Social Services and Community Committee Social Security (Accident Compensation and Calculation of Weekly Income) Amendment Bill Hearing of evidence John Miller Law Merlyn Remiens, Lawyer Alexandra Hodgson Hazel Armstrong (Armstrong Thompson Law)
descriptionSummary: Social Security (Accident Compensation and Calculation of Weekly Income) Amendment Bill. John Miller Law. Merlyn Remiens, Lawyer

chunks.text: So we are continuing to hear submissions this afternoon from the Social Security Accident Compensation and calculation of Weekly Income Amendment Bill. And our first, this afternoon is, uh, John Miller Law. Um, good afternoon, Milan. Believe you're good afternoon. Can you guys see and hear me all right? We can. Uh, great. Thank you. Fantastic.

Four 20 this afternoon. We have 10 minutes with you. Um, so when you're ready, um, take it away and if you wanna leave any type of questions, uh, please feel free to do so. Brilliant. I will certainly attempt to do so. So, my name is Merlin Ians, as noted here on behalf of John Miller Law and the A CC Futures Coalition, which itself is a multidisciplinary panel of interest in professionals and service providers for a CC.

Now, I was watching some of the earlier submissions, and I noted that the question of where exactly is this inequity kept popping up. So I just wanted to provide an example, a practical example of what exactly that might be. So let's use the example of persons X and Y. Now, both of these people were injured at the same time, and a CC coverable circumstances, person X receives their a CC cover and Person Y does not.

And this state of affair continues for five years and will identify those five years as starting at point A, being the date of injury and ending at point B, being the date when person wise cover is recognized. And at that stage, at point B, both of them are on equal footing. Now, person X who does have a CC cover receives as their main benefit.

So we'll usually terms of the bill here for clarity, uh, low or loss of potential earnings set at 80% of minimum wage. And they receive that for the full five years. Now, as their supplementary entitlements, they receive, for example, rehabilitation in the form of, uh, therapy or of physiotherapy or other clinical interventions.

They receive vocational rehab so that they can either return to their pre-injury role or adapt to their new normal and find a new role. And they can receive adjunct or supplementary entitlements such as, uh, transportation, home health, even educational support. And that's because they were appropriately assessed and were triaged into the correct, uh, ministerial role.

Uh, plus, and y on the other hand, was subject to an era of some kind. They did not receive a CC support when they should have, and it took those five years between point A and point B for that to be established. Now, for those five years, that person received the main benefit or, or living systems under MSD to the tune of 52% of the minimum wage.

And their supplementary benefits were, say, accommodation, which you only get if you need it. Winter energy support, which I understand isn't means tested, but amounts only to about $500 a year. And due to the type of injury they suffered, uh, access to disability support, which with which they might pay for their own medication, transport therapy, et cetera.

And as I say, this continues for five years. And the resolution here is what we're really concerned with is at point B when both persons are now viewed as equal under the law. Person X who received their support the whole time as they were due, has been rehabilitated.

They've been receiving this higher amount the whole time. They have likely returned to work or otherwise been sufficiently rehabilitated to return to an alternative role. But person y who has been the unfortunate victim of an error and has been receiving a lower quantum for those four, five years, is essentially starting to gain from the beginning.

They are now receiving their rehabilitation support. They're now receiving their vocational entitlements. And this bill seeks to treat those people retrospectively, as always having been on the same footing. And that, I think is the inequity that is being highlighted by the submitters, is that regardless of whether or not the bill itself applies retrospectively, the conclusion to the bill will always inherently be retrospective.

It is deeming a state of affairs that did not, in fact occur to have occurred over a period of time and punishing the persons, um, not entitled at first instance, to a CC support for their inability to either tell the future or to convince a ministry without years of often quite difficult litigation that they were in fact entitled to it in the first place.

Now, that actually brings me to my second point, which is that this highlights an inconsistency between the way the proposed bill administers, um, with, with regard to errors and the way the current legislation does. So let's view the reverse situation where somebody who should not have received a CC cover does in fact get it. If they had a congenital mental injury, for example, that was misdiagnosed as a consequence of trauma and cover was awarded, they could access this vastly more expensive rehabilitation.

They could access all of these supplementary assistances such as, uh, education and vocational rehabilitation. And corporation has a second look and says, sorry, we shouldn't have awarded this. We're revoking it. They get to keep all of those supplementary assistance items. There is, even though they were receiving perhaps lots of potential earnings at a much higher rate than they would've otherwise been receiving the m SD benefit, they get to keep it specifically because there was no element of wrongdoing on their part.

Now, the same thing happens if this was merely an administrative error, if it was deemed cover where say a statutory timeframe hasn't been met. So through no fault of either the party, no fault of a c, no fault of the recipient, they do nonetheless receive save in a surgical entitlement or personal impairment compensation, which can be to the tune of several hundreds of thousands of dollars, uh, unless there was an element of wrongdoing, unless there was fraud or misleading, they get to keep that.

And there is no rational reason why a more injured party who is being more significantly disadvantaged should at the resolution of substantially similar proceedings, be disadvantaged by this bill, particularly when that comes inherently retrospective effect, not only built into the bill, but into the actual structure of these debt repayments themselves.

But because there's simply no rationale to it. Now, the last part that I'll just very quickly make is that the intent as we see it of this bill is to, for the crown to reclaim money from citizens. 'cause otherwise, it's simply a, a debt balancing act between Crown agencies and with respect even that goal, we don't see as actually being satisfied by the proposed bill.

So to make these figures a bit more real, I'll just use those in the case of b, where this person had been appropriately receiving MSD support instead of a CC support for a period of 13 years. The debt at issue was only $14,000 over those years. That was settled initially in 2019 and only came to a head in 2005.

Being six years further of ongoing litigation during which there will have been costs paid by the Ministry of Justice to the courts and to representatives, that person will no doubt have been receiving ongoing clinical assessments in order to verify the kind of support that they needed under a CC. And as I say, during those 13 years, they were not receiving the initial rehabilitation that they needed.

So I suggest that the, as I believe one of the earliest submitters, um, Reitan suggested that the appropriate efficient solution isn't to try and put a, a financial barrier to the ambulance at the bottom of the cliff, but rather to ensure that these kinds of administrative errors are not made at first instance. Um, the last thing that I wish to point out on that is that the new proposed bill would actually implement financial consequences for the partners of these injured people as well, which do not presently exist

under the act is currently constructed. And the bill itself is replete with must clauses. MSD must assess. MSD must look into this, that itself is a significant administrative burden for a return that may not actually be worth it in the end, particularly when you take into account the fact that a CC will be involved at the end there.

There'll be more counsel if that person needs representation because of the financial status, they're likely to get a Ministry of Justice grant, et cetera, et cetera. We're dealing with relatively low recovery fig figures and relatively high cost of getting those, uh, that recovery back to the crown. So in my written submission, we've included a brief example of how this bill might be more effectively enacted as a debt zeroing exercise between ministries, including, actually this was not something that I'd thought of earlier

that, that Ms. Burton brought up that IRD actually needs to be involved as well, because if MSD is claiming that the gross cost and not the net, then the government is themselves double dipping being the very misstep that they accused these victims of, of sexual assault to be doing. I do apologize if that took too long, but I welcome any questions.

Uh, thank you Mil, we yep. Uh, we almost have time, but look, uh, one, uh, Ricardo, quickly, Thank you very much for your both, uh, your two examples. Hey, um, the k kind of case studies that you gave us for hypothetical case studies, um, the second one that took five years, what kind of demographic profile would you be looking at that would normally take five years to be able to get their a CC claim? Who are we talking about?

Certainly the best example that I can probably give and the most common one across my desk personally are often victims of sexual assault who have an element of physical injury as well due to the violence with which the assault was committed. Now, there can often be a bit of a dispute between the interplay of physical injuries arising from that, uh, those events and mental injuries, which the corporation will often reasonably or otherwise, uh, consider to disentitle that person.

Because if they can't tell the origin of the incapacity, they have reasonable grounds to say, oh, we shouldn't be covering it. Now, that person may well be languishing under MSD support and not receiving rehabilitation for the five years. It takes their case to progress through the various physicians, et cetera, until we reach somebody who's sufficiently experienced to say, this is where this comes from, cover should or should not be awarded.

Thank you. Uh, Helen, we question? Sorry. Um, I thought we'd run out of time, but thank you. It's an amazing submission. Um, and I just wanted to talk to you about the must clause, um, because I take your point. Do you think that we could, when you look at it, could we replace that with some form of discretion that might look us, let us look at this kind of situation?

Absolutely. I think that worths bill to proceed, which I suggest isn't wise, that adding an element of discretion here would certainly be almost necessary to ensure it's administrative viability, because otherwise you are going to end up with a team of people looking into all of the financial consequences for the claimant themselves. Any partners, if it's been 13 years, there might be multiple partners who are all beneficiaries, who would be affected, et cetera.

So an element of discretion there is certainly useful in terms of ballparking the ultimate recovery figure and saying, Hey, this isn't worth it. Thank you. Thank you very much Merlin for, uh, making that submission this afternoon. Appreciate it. All right. Our next, uh, submitter is, uh, Alexandra, Alexandra Hoon.

You there? Alexandra, if, just turn on your camera and uh, press the unmute button. Sorry. I'm just, oh, There we go. So look, the time is yours when you're ready to go. Okay. I'll start on what I have ready and any questions at the end. Um, I'm opposed for the proposed insertion of section 1 9 8 A into the Social Security Acts of 2018. I wish to make it clear that I accept that long standing policies that we, a person receives a main benefit for a period later covered by backdated a CC compensation.

The main benefit amount should be refunded under section 2 5 2 of the Accident Compensation Act 2001. My concern is not about the repayments of the main benefits of income to support of benefit, which in some cases can be cre created over many years. My case is five.

Um, others I'm, I'm aware of, have a longer timeframe. The question you have to ask is, how can this happen? In my case, five review hearings and one court hearing later, all in my favor, this took an enormous amount of time and legal costs to both parties were enormous.

In my case, A CC um, had to cover the costs because the costs were awarded. My concern is about the extending recovery to specific supplementary assistance, including accommodation, supplement, temporary additional support, disability allowance, and went, uh, winter energy payments.

These payments are not based income replacements. They're needs based assessments at the point in time for a person that urgently requires assistance to meet the base basic essential living costs, such as rent power and any disability related expenses when they based income is insufficient.

When I was employed, I was a, I was paid the minimum wage rate. Now, after five years, that rate is at 80% of the minimum wage rate. It isn't that a contradiction in itself, minimum is minimum, but not where a CC is concerned. It seems to be very one-sided. Why this amendment raises fairness concerns.

When a CC delays or under recognizes any entitlements, individuals often experience genuine financial hardship during that period. Supplementary assistance is provided because the person does not have enough income to meet the basic living costs. Accommodation, supplement and temporary additional support are assessed based on actual income and actual housing costs during the relevant period of time.

The need was real at the time of the assistant was granted living on breadline, waiting for a CC to make their decision because the process is lengthy is and is not mentally conjunctive with, with claimants backdated, a CC mental, uh, backdated, a CC compensation corrects an income underpayment.

It does not change the fact that at the time the person did not have the access to their income opportunities lost to that person. Should they be charged ac should they be charging a CC use of money interest similar to what IRD does because the funds were rightly there and they have lost the enjoyment that goes were being paid correctly.

The a CC scheme is intended to provide timely and effective compensation for injury where delays occur, individual individuals should not face addit additional financial liability because the system does not operate as intended, as I've mentioned in my case, was five year period, and others is longer reassessing and recovering that assistance retrospectively treats individuals if they had had income that they did not have turning that support into debt after the fact risks undermining the purpose of the social security system.

The social security framework exists to prevent hardship and ensure essential living costs can be met when the income is insufficient. Supplementary assistance is designed to respond to real-time needs, retrospect, recovery requests weakens the protective function. The key distinctions, repayment of the main benefit preserves the principle of income substitution.

Recovery of supplementary assistant goes further. It converts genuine hardship support into retrospective debt, even where the support reflected real need at that time. There is a mental health side to all of this, and most people who are going through this same process with a CC or private providers not only suffer from their injuries, but also suffer from mental health illnesses caused by long-term drawn out processes.

And as in my case, I said before five years, if I didn't have the mental or financial support from my father, who knows the mental state I would've been in, would I have been, would I have had the ability to fight a CC and its associates for so long or what I've given up like most people do when they're worn out by the process?

Now, where is the fairness in that? Not that long ago, IRD undertook a harsh debt recovery regime, and one of the outcomes to this was people taking their own lives. And we were gonna see another government department repeating history all over again due to mental health issues created by this very debacle.

If re reconciliation between a CC and MSD is required, this should occur at a government accounting level rather than imposing additional debt on injured individuals. Lexander, we we are over time. How, how much more have you got? We do have your written submission as well. Um, just three lines. Okay, thanks.

I, I recommend that section 1 9 8 A not be enacted or if enacted, it should exclude recovery of specifics, but supplementary assistance with where hardship arose due to delays or underpaid a CC entitlements. Remember, this is called by a ccc, not the claimant. Public confidence in both a CC and MSD depends on fairness.

Injured people should not bear the cost of institutional delays. And that's me. No thank, thank you, Andrew. Good afternoon, Hazel. We can see you. Could you say something else or just couldn't hear you straight? Yes. Hello. We got excellent. Thank you. Look good Afternoon. Welcome. Thank you very much. Select committee. Um, so I want to particularly address the question of social rehabilitation and treatment.

So a person gets weekly compensation at 80% of their pre-injury earnings, or as of the case of, um, Alexandra, who you've just met, she was, um, on the uplift, which is 80% of the, um, minimum wage. Um, and in the a CC scheme, social rehabilitation is not paid out or treatment is not paid out of the weekly compensation.

It's an entitlement sitting above weekly compensation. So the act provides for weekly compensation, rehabilitation, um, as well as treatment. So you've got various entitlements and they're not traded off against each other. The the issue that I see is that, um, social rehabilitation, which a person might obtain when they're not covered by a CC, uh, will be mainly gained out of the disability allowance as I understand it.

Uh, but then they're expected under this bill, as I understand this bill, to then reimburse, um, MSD for the social rehabilitation that they've received, uh, and also the treatment. Um, and so it, it's not a, a double dipping as I see it. Um, so the people will be required to use their arrears of weekly compensation to reimburse the cost of social rehabilitation that otherwise would've been funded by a CC for a person to be eligible for, uh, social rehabilitation and treatment for that matter.

There is a process, um, they have to apply for it. Uh, they have to be assessed as needing it due to the covered injury. Um, and there has to be prior approval before the funding. So, and then there's a discretion, uh, sitting in there as well. So there's quite a robust process, uh, for obtaining social rehabilitation and treatment.

Uh, my suggestion, which I have at the, um, end of my very brief submission is that if, uh, MSD is desperate for the money, that they don't take it from the individual, uh, claimant, that they seek the reimbursement from a CC themselves, um, rather than from the person outta their weekly compensation.

So I, I, I kind of resist the concept that it's a double dipping, and I I wouldn't mind if we could have some time to discuss whether or not, actually it really is a double dipping. Um, I think Helen White asked a question about discretion in the a CC legislation.

There's a waiver provision, um, in relation to overpayment, and I'm suggesting that there needs to be some discretion, um, here, uh, whether you try and recover, um, issues, uh, payments that were made, uh, under the heading of treatment and, uh, social rehabilitation.

Another suggestion that I saw from, um, another submitter was that there was a cap on recovery, for example, $2,000, but personally, I don't think there should have to be a reimbursement for, uh, medical costs and rehabilitation. So I'm being very brief to allow us to have some opportunity for some questions.

Thank you. Well, thank you, Bazel. I appreciate that submission. Uh, we, we have first question from Ricardo. Thank you very much. Just two quick questions.
